case_name: Constance v Ministry of Defence & Anor [2020] EWHC 3029 (QB) (11 November 2020)

summary.brief: Medical malpractice case involving obstetrics gynaecology.

evidence_index.text: Mr Warren Constance, who was a serving soldier in the Royal Artillery from 18 January 1993 until his enforced medical retirement from the Army on 10 August 2011, claims damages arising out of the failure of two doctors
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The negligence on the part of both the doctors and the MoD is partially admitted but there are considerable disputes between the parties about what would have happened to Mr Constance if he had been given proper advice
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Constance was born on 31 July 1968 and grew up in Cornwall. After school he went into the building trade. He enlisted in the Army on 18 January 1993 when aged 24.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Caldera correctly diagnosed that Mr Constance was suffering from moderate form of conductive hearing loss.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: All of the experts agreed that an ENT surgeon should discuss both options with the patient and that it is negligent not to do so.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: There is no reference in the notes made by Mr Caldera in any of the consultations where he saw Mr Constance to clinical treatment options for the treatment of his otosclerosis being discussed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Mr Constance's evidence was that, although he could not precisely remember what was said to him in consultations with Mr Caldera, no one ever explained to him that he was suffering from conductive hearing loss
evidence_index.speaker: Mr Constance

evidence_index.text: I find that Mr Caldera had breached his duty of care to Mr Constance by failing to discuss the option of surgery with him during, at latest, the appointment in January 2005
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The reference to Mr Constance being 'restricted from noise exposure and firing weapons' can only have been based on an assumption that Mr Constance was suffering from NIHL
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The expert evidence confirmed that there is no good reason to impose these restrictions on a soldier who is suffering from conductive hearing loss.
evidence_index.speaker: Expert testimony

evidence_index.text: The MoD have accepted that the error in attributing his hearing loss to NIHL constituted negligence.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: I thus consider that the losses suffered by Mr Constance were the exclusive responsibility of the MoD in the period mid-2006 to mid-2008, and thereafter both the Trust and the MoD are jointly responsible
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

patient_demographics.relevant_social_history: Serving soldier in the Royal Artillery

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Mr. Constance reported gradual hearing loss over 5 years, unable to hear conversation in his right ear.

focused_exam.ears: Both tympanic membranes looked normal, no evidence of middle ear disease or effusion.

critical_findings: Conductive hearing loss worse on the right

requestables.name: ENT Consultation
requestables.description: Consultation with ENT specialist regarding hearing loss

reveal.clinical_significance: Confirms the type of hearing loss
reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt discussion of treatment options

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient diagnosed with conductive hearing loss
decision_points.prompt: What treatment options should be discussed with the patient?

options.description: Discuss both hearing aids and stapedectomy
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to discuss all viable treatment options
Failed to provide full information on treatment options

expected_action_court.description: Both hearing aids and stapedectomy should have been discussed

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failed to discuss surgical option which could have improved hearing
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Patient should have been informed of all treatment options

patient_outcome.description: Patient experienced prolonged hearing loss and psychiatric symptoms
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Loss of congenial employment, psychiatric condition

key_findings: Failure to discuss surgical option constituted negligence
Causation established - earlier intervention could have improved hearing

factual_timeline.event: ENT consultation diagnosing conductive hearing loss

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by not discussing stapedectomy
